question:
5. Show that the line which moves parallel to the plane \( y=z \) and which intersects the two parabolas \( y^{2}=2 x, z=0 \) and \( z^{2}=3 x, y=0 \) sweeps out the surface
\[
x=(y-z)\left(\frac{y}{2}-\frac{z}{3}\right) .
\]
solution:
Solution. Suppose that the line meets the first parabola at \( \left(18 s^{2}, 6 s, 0\right) \) and the second parabola at a distinct point \( \left(12 t^{2}, 0,6 t\right) \). The condition that the line be parallel to the plane \( y=z \) is that the direction vector of the line, namely
\[
\left(18 s^{2}-12 t^{2}, 6 s,-6 t\right)
\]
should lie in that plane, i.e., \( 6 s=-6 t \). Hence the two points are \( \left(18 t^{2}\right. \), \( -6 t, 0) \) and \( \left(12 t^{2}, 0,6 t\right) \), where \( t \neq 0 \).

This line has the parametric representation
\[
\left(18 t^{2},-6 t, 0\right)+u\left(6 t^{2},-6 t,-6 t\right)
\]
where \( u \) is the parameter. So we have the two-parameter representation
\[
\begin{array}{l}
x=18 t^{2}+6 u t^{2} \\
y=-6 t-6 u t \\
z=-6 t u
\end{array}
\]
where \( t \neq 0 \), for the surface generated by the moving line.
We eliminate the parameters by noting that
\[
y-z=-6 t, \quad \text { and }
\]
\[
\frac{y}{2}-\frac{z}{3}=-t(3+u) .
\]

So we get
\[
x=6 t(3 t+u t)=(y-z)\left(\frac{y}{2}-\frac{z}{3}\right) .
\]

This proves that any point on any line parallel to the plane \( y=z \) which meets the two given parabolas at distinct points lies on the surface (3).

Conversely, if \( \left(x_{1}, y_{1}, z_{1}\right) \) is a point on surface (3), with \( y_{1} \neq z_{1} \), the numbers \( t \) and \( u \) can be determined from (2) so that \( \left(x_{1}, y_{1}, z_{1}\right) \) has the form (1). Points on the plane \( y=z \), however, are special because the two given parabolas intersect the plane at the point \( (0,0,0) \). So every point of the plane is on some line which is parallel to (in fact contained in) the plane and which intersects both parabolas. If these lines are to be taken into account, then the locus (3) must be supplemented by adjoining the whole plane \( y=z \). The surface (3) itself meets the plane \( y=z \) in just one line, \( x=0, y=z \).

descriptive_long.question:
5. Show that the line which moves parallel to the plane \( coordy=coordz \) and which intersects the two parabolas \( coordy^{2}=2 coordx, coordz=0 \) and \( coordz^{2}=3 coordx, coordy=0 \) sweeps out the surface
\[
coordx=(coordy-coordz)\left(\frac{coordy}{2}-\frac{coordz}{3}\right) .
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Suppose that the line meets the first parabola at \( \left(18 sliding^{2}, 6 sliding, 0\right) \) and the second parabola at a distinct point \( \left(12 variable^{2}, 0,6 variable\right) \). The condition that the line be parallel to the plane \( coordy=coordz \) is that the direction vector of the line, namely
\[
\left(18 sliding^{2}-12 variable^{2}, 6 sliding,-6 variable\right)
\]
should lie in that plane, i.e., \( 6 sliding=-6 variable \). Hence the two points are \( \left(18 variable^{2}\right. \), \( -6 variable, 0) \) and \( \left(12 variable^{2}, 0,6 variable\right) \), where \( variable \neq 0 \).

This line has the parametric representation
\[
\left(18 variable^{2},-6 variable, 0\right)+parameter\left(6 variable^{2},-6 variable,-6 variable\right)
\]
where \( parameter \) is the parameter. So we have the two-parameter representation
\[
\begin{array}{l}
coordx=18 variable^{2}+6 parameter variable^{2} \\
coordy=-6 variable-6 parameter variable \\
coordz=-6 variable parameter
\end{array}
\]
where \( variable \neq 0 \), for the surface generated by the moving line.
We eliminate the parameters by noting that
\[
coordy-coordz=-6 variable, \quad \text { and }
\]
\[
\frac{coordy}{2}-\frac{coordz}{3}=- variable(3+parameter) .
\]

So we get
\[
coordx=6 variable(3 variable+parameter variable)=(coordy-coordz)\left(\frac{coordy}{2}-\frac{coordz}{3}\right) .
\]

This proves that any point on any line parallel to the plane \( coordy=coordz \) which meets the two given parabolas at distinct points lies on the surface (3).

Conversely, if \( \left(pointx, pointy, pointz\right) \) is a point on surface (3), with \( pointy \neq pointz \), the numbers \( variable \) and \( parameter \) can be determined from (2) so that \( \left(pointx, pointy, pointz\right) \) has the form (1). Points on the plane \( coordy=coordz \), however, are special because the two given parabolas intersect the plane at the point \( (0,0,0) \). So every point of the plane is on some line which is parallel to (in fact contained in) the plane and which intersects both parabolas. If these lines are to be taken into account, then the locus (3) must be supplemented by adjoining the whole plane \( coordy=coordz \). The surface (3) itself meets the plane \( coordy=coordz \) in just one line, \( coordx=0, coordy=coordz \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
5. Show that the line which moves parallel to the plane \( lanterncat=raspberry \) and which intersects the two parabolas \( lanterncat^{2}=2 meadowfox, raspberry=0 \) and \( raspberry^{2}=3 meadowfox, lanterncat=0 \) sweeps out the surface
\[
meadowfox=(lanterncat-raspberry)\left(\frac{lanterncat}{2}-\frac{raspberry}{3}\right) .
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Suppose that the line meets the first parabola at \( \left(18 copperant^{2}, 6 copperant, 0\right) \) and the second parabola at a distinct point \( \left(12 velvetlion^{2}, 0,6 velvetlion\right) \). The condition that the line be parallel to the plane \( lanterncat=raspberry \) is that the direction vector of the line, namely
\[
\left(18 copperant^{2}-12 velvetlion^{2}, 6 copperant,-6 velvetlion\right)
\]
should lie in that plane, i.e., \( 6 copperant=-6 velvetlion \). Hence the two points are \( \left(18 velvetlion^{2}\right. \), \( -6 velvetlion, 0) \) and \( \left(12 velvetlion^{2}, 0,6 velvetlion\right) \), where \( velvetlion \neq 0 \).

This line has the parametric representation
\[
\left(18 velvetlion^{2},-6 velvetlion, 0\right)+marblehare\left(6 velvetlion^{2},-6 velvetlion,-6 velvetlion\right)
\]
where \( marblehare \) is the parameter. So we have the two-parameter representation
\[
\begin{array}{l}
meadowfox=18 velvetlion^{2}+6 marblehare velvetlion^{2} \\
lanterncat=-6 velvetlion-6 marblehare velvetlion \\
raspberry=-6 velvetlion marblehare
\end{array}
\]
where \( velvetlion \neq 0 \), for the surface generated by the moving line.
We eliminate the parameters by noting that
\[
lanterncat-raspberry=-6 velvetlion, \quad \text { and }
\]
\[
\frac{lanterncat}{2}-\frac{raspberry}{3}=-velvetlion(3+marblehare) .
\]

So we get
\[
meadowfox=6 velvetlion(3 velvetlion+marblehare velvetlion)=(lanterncat-raspberry)\left(\frac{lanterncat}{2}-\frac{raspberry}{3}\right) .
\]

This proves that any point on any line parallel to the plane \( lanterncat=raspberry \) which meets the two given parabolas at distinct points lies on the surface (3).

Conversely, if \( \left(silenthawk, thunderpig, oceanmoss\right) \) is a point on surface (3), with \( thunderpig \neq oceanmoss \), the numbers \( velvetlion \) and \( marblehare \) can be determined from (2) so that \( \left(silenthawk, thunderpig, oceanmoss\right) \) has the form (1). Points on the plane \( lanterncat=raspberry \), however, are special because the two given parabolas intersect the plane at the point \( (0,0,0) \). So every point of the plane is on some line which is parallel to (in fact contained in) the plane and which intersects both parabolas. If these lines are to be taken into account, then the locus (3) must be supplemented by adjoining the whole plane \( lanterncat=raspberry \). The surface (3) itself meets the plane \( lanterncat=raspberry \) in just one line, \( meadowfox=0, lanterncat=raspberry \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
5. Show that the line which moves parallel to the plane \( horizontalaxis=surfacelayer \) and which intersects the two parabolas \( horizontalaxis^{2}=2 verticalaxis, surfacelayer=0 \) and \( surfacelayer^{2}=3 verticalaxis, horizontalaxis=0 \) sweeps out the surface
\[
verticalaxis=(horizontalaxis-surfacelayer)\left(\frac{horizontalaxis}{2}-\frac{surfacelayer}{3}\right) .
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Suppose that the line meets the first parabola at \( \left(18 endpoint^{2}, 6 endpoint, 0\right) \) and the second parabola at a distinct point \( \left(12 timelessness^{2}, 0,6 timelessness\right) \). The condition that the line be parallel to the plane \( horizontalaxis=surfacelayer \) is that the direction vector of the line, namely
\[
\left(18 endpoint^{2}-12 timelessness^{2}, 6 endpoint,-6 timelessness\right)
\]
should lie in that plane, i.e., \( 6 endpoint=-6 timelessness \). Hence the two points are \( \left(18 timelessness^{2}\right. , -6 timelessness, 0) \) and \( \left(12 timelessness^{2}, 0,6 timelessness\right) \), where \( timelessness \neq 0 \).

This line has the parametric representation
\[
\left(18 timelessness^{2},-6 timelessness, 0\right)+stagnation\left(6 timelessness^{2},-6 timelessness,-6 timelessness\right)
\]
where \( stagnation \) is the parameter. So we have the two-parameter representation
\[
\begin{array}{l}
verticalaxis=18 timelessness^{2}+6 stagnation timelessness^{2} \\
horizontalaxis=-6 timelessness-6 stagnation timelessness \\
surfacelayer=-6 timelessness stagnation
\end{array}
\]
where \( timelessness \neq 0 \), for the surface generated by the moving line.
We eliminate the parameters by noting that
\[
horizontalaxis-surfacelayer=-6 timelessness, \quad \text { and }
\]
\[
\frac{horizontalaxis}{2}-\frac{surfacelayer}{3}=-timelessness(3+stagnation) .
\]

So we get
\[
verticalaxis=6 timelessness(3 timelessness+stagnation timelessness)=(horizontalaxis-surfacelayer)\left(\frac{horizontalaxis}{2}-\frac{surfacelayer}{3}\right) .
\]

This proves that any point on any line parallel to the plane \( horizontalaxis=surfacelayer \) which meets the two given parabolas at distinct points lies on the surface (3).

Conversely, if \( \left(verticalaxispivot, horizontalaxispivot, surfacelayerpivot\right) \) is a point on surface (3), with \( horizontalaxispivot \neq surfacelayerpivot \), the numbers \( timelessness \) and \( stagnation \) can be determined from (2) so that \( \left(verticalaxispivot, horizontalaxispivot, surfacelayerpivot\right) \) has the form (1). Points on the plane \( horizontalaxis=surfacelayer \), however, are special because the two given parabolas intersect the plane at the point \( (0,0,0) \). So every point of the plane is on some line which is parallel to (in fact contained in) the plane and which intersects both parabolas. If these lines are to be taken into account, then the locus (3) must be supplemented by adjoining the whole plane \( horizontalaxis=surfacelayer \). The surface (3) itself meets the plane \( horizontalaxis=surfacelayer \) in just one line, \( verticalaxis=0, horizontalaxis=surfacelayer \).

garbled_string.question:
5. Show that the line which moves parallel to the plane \( hjgrksla=mvdprqoe \) and which intersects the two parabolas \( hjgrksla^{2}=2 qzxwvtnp, mvdprqoe=0 \) and \( mvdprqoe^{2}=3 qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla=0 \) sweeps out the surface
\[
qzxwvtnp=(hjgrksla-mvdprqoe)\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{2}-\frac{mvdprqoe}{3}\right) .
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Suppose that the line meets the first parabola at \( \left(18 atosirle^{2}, 6 atosirle, 0\right) \) and the second parabola at a distinct point \( \left(12 jrvumkeh^{2}, 0,6 jrvumkeh\right) \). The condition that the line be parallel to the plane \( hjgrksla=mvdprqoe \) is that the direction vector of the line, namely
\[
\left(18 atosirle^{2}-12 jrvumkeh^{2}, 6 atosirle,-6 jrvumkeh\right)
\]
should lie in that plane, i.e., \( 6 atosirle=-6 jrvumkeh \). Hence the two points are \( \left(18 jrvumkeh^{2}\right. , -6 jrvumkeh, 0) \) and \( \left(12 jrvumkeh^{2}, 0,6 jrvumkeh\right) \), where \( jrvumkeh \neq 0 \).

This line has the parametric representation
\[
\left(18 jrvumkeh^{2},-6 jrvumkeh, 0\right)+fqdwnpsi\left(6 jrvumkeh^{2},-6 jrvumkeh,-6 jrvumkeh\right)
\]
where \( fqdwnpsi \) is the parameter. So we have the two-parameter representation
\[
\begin{array}{l}
qzxwvtnp=18 jrvumkeh^{2}+6 fqdwnpsi jrvumkeh^{2} \\
hjgrksla=-6 jrvumkeh-6 fqdwnpsi jrvumkeh \\
mvdprqoe=-6 jrvumkeh fqdwnpsi
\end{array}
\]
where \( jrvumkeh \neq 0 \), for the surface generated by the moving line.
We eliminate the parameters by noting that
\[
hjgrksla-mvdprqoe=-6 jrvumkeh, \quad \text { and }
\]
\[
\frac{hjgrksla}{2}-\frac{mvdprqoe}{3}=-jrvumkeh(3+fqdwnpsi) .
\]

So we get
\[
qzxwvtnp=6 jrvumkeh(3 jrvumkeh+fqdwnpsi jrvumkeh)=(hjgrksla-mvdprqoe)\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{2}-\frac{mvdprqoe}{3}\right) .
\]

This proves that any point on any line parallel to the plane \( hjgrksla=mvdprqoe \) which meets the two given parabolas at distinct points lies on the surface (3).

Conversely, if \( \left(lqnfsmda, swvpkjrh, cegxdnoh\right) \) is a point on surface (3), with \( swvpkjrh \neq cegxdnoh \), the numbers \( jrvumkeh \) and \( fqdwnpsi \) can be determined from (2) so that \( \left(lqnfsmda, swvpkjrh, cegxdnoh\right) \) has the form (1). Points on the plane \( hjgrksla=mvdprqoe \), however, are special because the two given parabolas intersect the plane at the point \( (0,0,0) \). So every point of the plane is on some line which is parallel to (in fact contained in) the plane and which intersects both parabolas. If these lines are to be taken into account, then the locus (3) must be supplemented by adjoining the whole plane \( hjgrksla=mvdprqoe \). The surface (3) itself meets the plane \( hjgrksla=mvdprqoe \) in just one line, \( qzxwvtnp=0, hjgrksla=mvdprqoe \).

kernel_variant.question:
In the Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ consider the two parabolas  
\[
\mathcal{C}_{1}\colon z^{2}=5x,\quad y=0 ,
\qquad  
\mathcal{C}_{2}\colon y^{2}=7x,\quad z=0 .
\]

Let $\Pi$ be the plane $y+z=0$.  
A line $\ell$ is called \emph{admissible} if  

(a) $\ell$ meets $\mathcal{C}_{1}$ and $\mathcal{C}_{2}$ in two distinct points,  

(b) $\ell$ is parallel to $\Pi$, that is, its direction vector $v$ satisfies $v\!\cdot\!(0,1,1)=0$.  

Answer the following questions.

(i)  Prove that admissible lines exist and determine an explicit one-parameter family  
\[
\mathfrak{L}\;=\;\{\ell(\lambda)\mid \lambda\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}\}
\]
containing \emph{all} admissible lines.

(ii)  Write every $\ell(\lambda)$ in homogeneous Plucker coordinates  
\[
(p_{01}:p_{02}:p_{03}:p_{12}:p_{13}:p_{23})\in\mathbb{P}^{5},
\]
show that they satisfy both the Klein-quadric equation  
\[
p_{01}p_{23}+p_{02}p_{31}+p_{03}p_{12}=0 \tag{$\dagger$}
\]
and the three independent linear relations  
\begin{align}
p_{02}+p_{03}&=0, \tag{$\ddagger_{1}$}\\
5p_{12}+7p_{13}&=0, \tag{$\ddagger_{2}$}\\
35p_{01}-2p_{23}&=0. \tag{$\ddagger_{3}$}
\end{align}
Let  
\[
\Lambda\;:=\;\bigl\{(p)\in\mathbb{P}^{5}\bigm|\text{$(\ddagger_{1})$-$(\ddagger_{3})$ hold}\bigr\}\cong\mathbb{P}^{2},
\]
and let $Z$ be the intersection of $\Lambda$ with the Klein quadric, i.e. the common zero locus of $(\dagger)$ and $(\ddagger_{1})$-$(\ddagger_{3})$.

(1)  Prove that $Z$ is the \emph{disjoint} union of the following three subsets  

$\bullet$ the Zariski-open part  
\[
C\;:=\;\Bigl\{(p)\in Z\ \Bigm|\ p_{23}\neq0\Bigr\},
\]
which is a smooth conic $C\cong\mathbb{P}^{1}$ (whose points are exactly the lines of $\mathfrak{L}$),  

$\bullet$ the Plucker point $P_{L}=(0:1:-1:0:0:0)$ representing the generator  
\[
L\colon x=0,\; y+z=0,
\]

$\bullet$ the Plucker point $P_{\infty}=(0:0:0:1:-5/7:0)$ representing an ideal line contained in the plane at infinity.  

(2)  Show that neither $P_{L}$ nor $P_{\infty}$ corresponds to an admissible line.

(iii)  Eliminate the Plucker coordinates and show that the union of all admissible lines is  
\[
S\setminus L\quad\text{with}\quad
S\colon x=\dfrac{(y+z)(5y+7z)}{35}, \tag{$\star$}
\]
i.e. the quadric surface $S$ minus the generator  
\[
L\colon x=0,\;y+z=0\;=\;S\cap\Pi .
\]
Show that every affine point of $S$ that does \emph{not} lie on $L$ is contained in a unique admissible line, while $L$ is reached by no admissible line.

(iv)  Verify that $S$ is a hyperbolic paraboloid.  Determine explicitly the
second ruling  
\[
\widehat{\mathfrak{L}}\;=\;\{\widehat{\ell}(k)\mid k\in\mathbb{R}\},
\]
and prove that no $\widehat{\ell}(k)$ is parallel to $\Pi$ (hence no $\widehat{\ell}(k)$ is admissible), although every $\widehat{\ell}(k)$ meets $\mathcal{C}_{1}$ and $\mathcal{C}_{2}$.  (For $k=0$ the two intersections collapse to $(0,0,0)$, so $\widehat{\ell}(0)$ is not admissible.)

(v)  Regard $S$ as the regular surface $(y,z)\mapsto\bigl(f(y,z),y,z\bigr)$ with  
\[
f(y,z)=\dfrac{(y+z)(5y+7z)}{35}.
\]

(1)  Compute the first and second fundamental forms and show that the
Gaussian curvature is  
\[
K(y,z)\;=\;-\dfrac{4}{35^{2}}\bigl(1+\|\nabla f(y,z)\|^{2}\bigr)^{-2}\;<\;0
\quad\forall\,(y,z)\in\mathbb{R}^{2}.
\]

(2)  Conclude that $S$ is a nowhere developable ruled surface:
neither of its two rulings consists of developable directions.

(3)  Find the mean curvature $H(y,z)$ and prove that it vanishes
precisely for those $(y,z)$ that satisfy  
\[
5y^{2}+12yz+7z^{2}=3675. \tag{$\heartsuit$}
\]

Consequently the set of minimal points of $S$ is the space curve  
\[
\Xi:=\bigl\{\bigl(f(y,z),y,z\bigr)\bigm| (y,z)\text{ satisfy }(\heartsuit)\bigr\};
\]
in particular, the origin $(0,0,0)$ is not a minimal point of $S$.

\bigskip
kernel_variant.solution:
\textbf{Notation.}  Affinely embed $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ in projective space $\mathbb{P}^{3}$ with homogeneous coordinates $(w:x:y:z)$; $w\neq0$ corresponds to the usual point $(x,y,z)$.

\medskip
\underline{\textbf{Part (i) - Parametrisation of all admissible lines}}

Let $P$ be the intersection of an admissible line with $\mathcal{C}_{1}$ and $Q$ the intersection with $\mathcal{C}_{2}$.  Write
\[
P=(x_{1},0,z_{1}), \qquad z_{1}^{2}=5x_{1},\qquad 
Q=(x_{2},y_{2},0), \qquad y_{2}^{2}=7x_{2}.
\]
Because the line is parallel to $\Pi$ we must have $z_{1}=y_{2}=:\lambda$ with $\lambda\neq0$.  Consequently
\[
P=\Bigl(\dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{5},0,\lambda\Bigr),\qquad 
Q=\Bigl(\dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{7},\lambda,0\Bigr). \tag{1}
\]
The direction vector is
\[
v=Q-P=\Bigl(\dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{7}-\dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{5},\lambda,-\lambda\Bigr)
=\Bigl(-\dfrac{2\lambda^{2}}{35},\;\lambda,\;-\lambda\Bigr), \tag{2}
\]
and indeed $v\!\cdot\!(0,1,1)=0$.  Conversely, condition (b) forces $z_{1}=y_{2}$, so every admissible line arises from some $\lambda\neq0$.  Taking an affine parameter $u$ along the line yields
\begin{align}
\ell(\lambda)\colon\;(x,y,z)
&=\Bigl(\dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{5},0,\lambda\Bigr)
  +u\Bigl(-\dfrac{2\lambda^{2}}{35},\lambda,-\lambda\Bigr)\notag\\
&=\Bigl(\dfrac{\lambda^{2}(7-2u)}{35},\;\lambda u,\;\lambda(1-u)\Bigr).
\tag{3}
\end{align}
Thus
\[
\mathfrak{L}\;=\;\{\ell(\lambda)\mid\lambda\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}\}
\]
is exactly the set of admissible lines.

\bigskip
\underline{\textbf{Part (ii) - Plucker coordinates and the decomposition of $Z$}}

With homogeneous points $\bar{P}=(1:\lambda^{2}/5:0:\lambda)$ and $\bar{Q}=(1:\lambda^{2}/7:\lambda:0)$ the Plucker coordinates
$p_{ij}=\bar{P}_{i}\bar{Q}_{j}-\bar{P}_{j}\bar{Q}_{i}$ are
\[
\begin{aligned}
p_{01}&=-\dfrac{2\lambda^{2}}{35}, & p_{02}&=\lambda, & p_{03}&=-\lambda,\\
p_{12}&=\dfrac{\lambda^{3}}{5},    & p_{13}&=-\dfrac{\lambda^{3}}{7}, & p_{23}&=-\lambda^{2}. \tag{4}
\end{aligned}
\]
They satisfy $(\dagger)$ and $(\ddagger_{1})$-$(\ddagger_{3})$ by direct substitution.

\smallskip
Inside $\mathbb{P}^{5}$ the three independent linear equations cut out the projective plane  
\[
\Lambda\cong\mathbb{P}^{2}.
\]
Introduce homogeneous coordinates on $\Lambda$ by
\[
(u:v:w):=(p_{02}:p_{12}:p_{01}). \tag{5}
\]
Using $(\ddagger_{1})$-$(\ddagger_{3})$ we obtain
\[
p_{03}=-u,\qquad p_{13}=-\dfrac{5v}{7},\qquad p_{23}= \dfrac{35w}{2}. \tag{6}
\]
Substituting these expressions into $(\dagger)$ gives the quadratic
\[
\dfrac{35}{2}w^{2}-\dfrac{2}{7}uv=0, \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad 245w^{2}-4uv=0. \tag{7}
\]
Hence
\[
Z=\bigl\{(u:v:w)\in\mathbb{P}^{2}\bigm|245w^{2}-4uv=0\bigr\}. \tag{8}
\]

\textbf{Decomposition of $Z$.}  
Define
\[
C:=\bigl\{(u:v:w)\in Z\mid w\neq0\bigr\}. \tag{9}
\]
Because $w\neq0$ one can de-homogenise the quadratic equation to obtain an affine equation of a smooth conic; hence $C$ is smooth and isomorphic to $\mathbb{P}^{1}$.

The two points with $w=0$ are
\[
(1:0:0)\quad\text{and}\quad(0:1:0),
\]
corresponding (via (6)) to the Plucker points  
\[
P_{L}=(0:1:-1:0:0:0),\qquad
P_{\infty}=(0:0:0:1:-5/7:0).
\]
Thus
\[
Z=C\;\dot{\cup}\;\{P_{L}\}\;\dot{\cup}\;\{P_{\infty}\}. \tag{10}
\]
Employing $w=-2\lambda^{2}/35\neq0$ we obtain
\[
u=\lambda,\quad v=\dfrac{\lambda^{3}}{5},\quad w=-\dfrac{2\lambda^{2}}{35},
\]
which satisfies (7) and reproduces (4); consequently $C$ is exactly the
Plucker image of $\mathfrak{L}$ and no admissible line is represented by
$P_{L}$ or $P_{\infty}$.

\bigskip
\underline{\textbf{Part (iii) - The surface swept out by $\mathfrak{L}$}}

From (3) we have the relations
\[
y+z=\lambda,\qquad 5y+7z=\lambda(7-2u). \tag{11}
\]
Hence
\[
x=\dfrac{\lambda^{2}(7-2u)}{35}
     =\dfrac{(y+z)(5y+7z)}{35}, \tag{12}
\]
so every admissible line lies on
\[
S\colon x=\dfrac{(y+z)(5y+7z)}{35}. \tag{13}
\]
Conversely, let $(x,y,z)\in S$ with $(y,z)\neq(0,0)$.  Put
\[
\lambda:=y+z,\qquad u:=\dfrac{y}{\lambda}.
\]
Then $(x,y,z)$ coincides with (3).  Because $\lambda\neq0$, the point
lies on a \emph{unique} admissible line.  
Points of $L\colon x=0,\;y+z=0$ satisfy $\lambda=0$ and are not reached.  
Therefore
\[
\bigcup_{\ell\in\mathfrak{L}}\ell \;=\;S\setminus L. \tag{14}
\]

\bigskip
\underline{\textbf{Part (iv) - Hyperbolic paraboloid and the second ruling}}

Rewrite $(\star)$ as
\[
35x=(y+z)(5y+7z)=5y^{2}+12yz+7z^{2}. \tag{15}
\]
The quadratic form on the right has determinant $5\cdot7-6^{2}=-1<0$, hence $S$ is a hyperbolic paraboloid and therefore doubly ruled.

\smallskip
Fix $k\in\mathbb{R}$ and set
\[
y(t)=7t,\qquad z(t)=-5t+\dfrac{k}{7}. \tag{16}
\]
Then $5y(t)+7z(t)=k$ and $y(t)+z(t)=2t+k/7$, giving
\[
x(t)=\dfrac{(2t+k/7)k}{35}=\dfrac{2k}{35}t+\dfrac{k^{2}}{245}. \tag{17}
\]
Thus
\[
\widehat{\ell}(k)\colon
(x,y,z)=\Bigl(\dfrac{2k}{35}t+\dfrac{k^{2}}{245},\;7t,\;-5t+\dfrac{k}{7}\Bigr),
\quad t\in\mathbb{R}. \tag{18}
\]
The direction vector is $\widehat{v}=(2k/35,\,7,\,-5)$, and
$\widehat{v}\!\cdot\!(0,1,1)=2\neq0$, so no $\widehat{\ell}(k)$ is parallel to $\Pi$.  If $k\neq0$, (18) meets $\mathcal{C}_{1}$ at $t=0$ and $\mathcal{C}_{2}$ at $t=k/35$; for $k=0$ these merge to $(0,0,0)$.  Hence $\widehat{\mathfrak{L}}$ is the second ruling of $S$ and contains no admissible line.

\bigskip
\underline{\textbf{Part (v) - Differential-geometric properties}}

Put
\[
r(y,z)=\bigl(f(y,z),y,z\bigr),\qquad
f(y,z)=\dfrac{(y+z)(5y+7z)}{35}. \tag{19}
\]
\textbf{First derivatives.}  
\[
f_{y}=\dfrac{10y+12z}{35},\qquad 
f_{z}=\dfrac{12y+14z}{35}.
\]
Set $a:=f_{y}$, $b:=f_{z}$ and $W^{2}:=1+a^{2}+b^{2}$.

First fundamental form  
\[
E=1+a^{2},\qquad F=ab,\qquad G=1+b^{2}. \tag{20}
\]

\textbf{Second derivatives.}  
\[
f_{yy}=\dfrac{2}{7},\qquad f_{yz}=\dfrac{12}{35},\qquad f_{zz}=\dfrac{2}{5}. \tag{21}
\]

Second fundamental coefficients (with unit normal $N=(1,-a,-b)/W$):
\[
e=\dfrac{f_{yy}}{W},\qquad 
f=\dfrac{f_{yz}}{W},\qquad 
g=\dfrac{f_{zz}}{W}. \tag{22}
\]

\textbf{Gaussian curvature.}  
\[
eg-f^{2}
=\dfrac{f_{yy}f_{zz}-f_{yz}^{2}}{W^{2}}
=-\dfrac{4}{35^{2}}\dfrac{1}{W^{2}},\qquad
EG-F^{2}=W^{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
K=\dfrac{eg-f^{2}}{EG-F^{2}}
=-\dfrac{4}{35^{2}}\dfrac{1}{W^{4}}
=-\dfrac{4}{35^{2}}\bigl(1+\|\nabla f\|^{2}\bigr)^{-2}<0\quad\forall(y,z). \tag{23}
\]

\textbf{Non-developability.}  
For a ruled surface, developability along a ruling would force $K\equiv0$ on that ruling, impossible by (23).  Thus neither ruling is developable and $S$ is nowhere developable.

\textbf{Mean curvature.}  For a graph $(x,y,z)=(f(u,v),u,v)$
\[
H=\dfrac{(1+b^{2})f_{yy}-2ab\,f_{yz}+(1+a^{2})f_{zz}}
         {2\bigl(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\bigr)^{3/2}}. \tag{24}
\]
Substituting the derivatives gives
\[
H(y,z)=\dfrac{12+7a^{2}+5b^{2}-12ab}
              {35\bigl(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\bigr)^{3/2}}. \tag{25}
\]
Clearing the denominator $35^{2}$ one finds
\[
H=0\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
5y^{2}+12yz+7z^{2}=3675. \tag{26}
\]
Because the quadratic form has determinant $-1$, (26) describes a real hyperbola in the $(y,z)$-plane.  Its image under $r$ is the space curve
\[
\Xi=\bigl\{\bigl(f(y,z),y,z\bigr)\mid5y^{2}+12yz+7z^{2}=3675\bigr\}. \tag{27}
\]
Thus the mean curvature vanishes exactly along $\Xi$; in particular $H(0,0)=12/35>0$, so $(0,0,0)$ is not a minimal point.

\hfill$\square$

\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Higher-level language.  The solution uses *projective geometry* and *Plücker coordinates*, tools absent from the original problem.

2.  Extra conditions.  Besides eliminating two parameters as in the kernel problem, the solver must cope with **three** algebraic constraints—the Klein quadric and the two linear relations (‡)—and interpret their incidence-geometric meaning.

3.  Multiple interacting concepts.  Classical analytic geometry (parametrising lines, eliminating parameters), projective/Grassmannian geometry (lines as points of the Klein quadric), algebraic-geometric reasoning (a conic as intersection of quadrics), and differential geometry (Gaussian curvature) all interact.

4.  Deeper theory required.  Identifying the geometry of S as a *hyperbolic paraboloid*, finding the *second ruling*, and computing its *Gaussian curvature* demand results well beyond the undergraduate analytic manipulations sufficient for the original exercise.

5.  More computations.  The Plücker-coordinate verification, elimination, curvature calculation, and ruling analysis pile several layers of non-trivial algebra onto the original two-parameter elimination.

Consequently the enhanced variant is substantially harder and cannot be solved by a single-line pattern match; it imposes sophisticated techniques from several advanced areas of geometry.

original_kernel_variant.question:
In the Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ consider the two parabolas  
\[
\mathcal{C}_{1}\colon z^{2}=5x,\quad y=0 ,
\qquad  
\mathcal{C}_{2}\colon y^{2}=7x,\quad z=0 .
\]

Let $\Pi$ be the plane $y+z=0$.  
A line $\ell$ is called \emph{admissible} if  

(a) $\ell$ meets $\mathcal{C}_{1}$ and $\mathcal{C}_{2}$ in two distinct points,  

(b) $\ell$ is parallel to $\Pi$, that is, its direction vector $v$ satisfies $v\!\cdot\!(0,1,1)=0$.  

Answer the following questions.

(i)  Prove that admissible lines exist and determine an explicit one-parameter family  
\[
\mathfrak{L}\;=\;\{\ell(\lambda)\mid \lambda\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}\}
\]
containing \emph{all} admissible lines.

(ii)  Write every $\ell(\lambda)$ in homogeneous Plucker coordinates  
\[
(p_{01}:p_{02}:p_{03}:p_{12}:p_{13}:p_{23})\in\mathbb{P}^{5},
\]
show that they satisfy both the Klein-quadric equation  
\[
p_{01}p_{23}+p_{02}p_{31}+p_{03}p_{12}=0 \tag{$\dagger$}
\]
and the three independent linear relations  
\begin{align}
p_{02}+p_{03}&=0, \tag{$\ddagger_{1}$}\\
5p_{12}+7p_{13}&=0, \tag{$\ddagger_{2}$}\\
35p_{01}-2p_{23}&=0. \tag{$\ddagger_{3}$}
\end{align}
Let  
\[
\Lambda\;:=\;\bigl\{(p)\in\mathbb{P}^{5}\bigm|\text{$(\ddagger_{1})$-$(\ddagger_{3})$ hold}\bigr\}\cong\mathbb{P}^{2},
\]
and let $Z$ be the intersection of $\Lambda$ with the Klein quadric, i.e. the common zero locus of $(\dagger)$ and $(\ddagger_{1})$-$(\ddagger_{3})$.

(1)  Prove that $Z$ is the \emph{disjoint} union of the following three subsets  

$\bullet$ the Zariski-open part  
\[
C\;:=\;\Bigl\{(p)\in Z\ \Bigm|\ p_{23}\neq0\Bigr\},
\]
which is a smooth conic $C\cong\mathbb{P}^{1}$ (whose points are exactly the lines of $\mathfrak{L}$),  

$\bullet$ the Plucker point $P_{L}=(0:1:-1:0:0:0)$ representing the generator  
\[
L\colon x=0,\; y+z=0,
\]

$\bullet$ the Plucker point $P_{\infty}=(0:0:0:1:-5/7:0)$ representing an ideal line contained in the plane at infinity.  

(2)  Show that neither $P_{L}$ nor $P_{\infty}$ corresponds to an admissible line.

(iii)  Eliminate the Plucker coordinates and show that the union of all admissible lines is  
\[
S\setminus L\quad\text{with}\quad
S\colon x=\dfrac{(y+z)(5y+7z)}{35}, \tag{$\star$}
\]
i.e. the quadric surface $S$ minus the generator  
\[
L\colon x=0,\;y+z=0\;=\;S\cap\Pi .
\]
Show that every affine point of $S$ that does \emph{not} lie on $L$ is contained in a unique admissible line, while $L$ is reached by no admissible line.

(iv)  Verify that $S$ is a hyperbolic paraboloid.  Determine explicitly the
second ruling  
\[
\widehat{\mathfrak{L}}\;=\;\{\widehat{\ell}(k)\mid k\in\mathbb{R}\},
\]
and prove that no $\widehat{\ell}(k)$ is parallel to $\Pi$ (hence no $\widehat{\ell}(k)$ is admissible), although every $\widehat{\ell}(k)$ meets $\mathcal{C}_{1}$ and $\mathcal{C}_{2}$.  (For $k=0$ the two intersections collapse to $(0,0,0)$, so $\widehat{\ell}(0)$ is not admissible.)

(v)  Regard $S$ as the regular surface $(y,z)\mapsto\bigl(f(y,z),y,z\bigr)$ with  
\[
f(y,z)=\dfrac{(y+z)(5y+7z)}{35}.
\]

(1)  Compute the first and second fundamental forms and show that the
Gaussian curvature is  
\[
K(y,z)\;=\;-\dfrac{4}{35^{2}}\bigl(1+\|\nabla f(y,z)\|^{2}\bigr)^{-2}\;<\;0
\quad\forall\,(y,z)\in\mathbb{R}^{2}.
\]

(2)  Conclude that $S$ is a nowhere developable ruled surface:
neither of its two rulings consists of developable directions.

(3)  Find the mean curvature $H(y,z)$ and prove that it vanishes
precisely for those $(y,z)$ that satisfy  
\[
5y^{2}+12yz+7z^{2}=3675. \tag{$\heartsuit$}
\]

Consequently the set of minimal points of $S$ is the space curve  
\[
\Xi:=\bigl\{\bigl(f(y,z),y,z\bigr)\bigm| (y,z)\text{ satisfy }(\heartsuit)\bigr\};
\]
in particular, the origin $(0,0,0)$ is not a minimal point of $S$.

\bigskip
original_kernel_variant.solution:
\textbf{Notation.}  Affinely embed $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ in projective space $\mathbb{P}^{3}$ with homogeneous coordinates $(w:x:y:z)$; $w\neq0$ corresponds to the usual point $(x,y,z)$.

\medskip
\underline{\textbf{Part (i) - Parametrisation of all admissible lines}}

Let $P$ be the intersection of an admissible line with $\mathcal{C}_{1}$ and $Q$ the intersection with $\mathcal{C}_{2}$.  Write
\[
P=(x_{1},0,z_{1}), \qquad z_{1}^{2}=5x_{1},\qquad 
Q=(x_{2},y_{2},0), \qquad y_{2}^{2}=7x_{2}.
\]
Because the line is parallel to $\Pi$ we must have $z_{1}=y_{2}=:\lambda$ with $\lambda\neq0$.  Consequently
\[
P=\Bigl(\dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{5},0,\lambda\Bigr),\qquad 
Q=\Bigl(\dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{7},\lambda,0\Bigr). \tag{1}
\]
The direction vector is
\[
v=Q-P=\Bigl(\dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{7}-\dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{5},\lambda,-\lambda\Bigr)
=\Bigl(-\dfrac{2\lambda^{2}}{35},\;\lambda,\;-\lambda\Bigr), \tag{2}
\]
and indeed $v\!\cdot\!(0,1,1)=0$.  Conversely, condition (b) forces $z_{1}=y_{2}$, so every admissible line arises from some $\lambda\neq0$.  Taking an affine parameter $u$ along the line yields
\begin{align}
\ell(\lambda)\colon\;(x,y,z)
&=\Bigl(\dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{5},0,\lambda\Bigr)
  +u\Bigl(-\dfrac{2\lambda^{2}}{35},\lambda,-\lambda\Bigr)\notag\\
&=\Bigl(\dfrac{\lambda^{2}(7-2u)}{35},\;\lambda u,\;\lambda(1-u)\Bigr).
\tag{3}
\end{align}
Thus
\[
\mathfrak{L}\;=\;\{\ell(\lambda)\mid\lambda\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}\}
\]
is exactly the set of admissible lines.

\bigskip
\underline{\textbf{Part (ii) - Plucker coordinates and the decomposition of $Z$}}

With homogeneous points $\bar{P}=(1:\lambda^{2}/5:0:\lambda)$ and $\bar{Q}=(1:\lambda^{2}/7:\lambda:0)$ the Plucker coordinates
$p_{ij}=\bar{P}_{i}\bar{Q}_{j}-\bar{P}_{j}\bar{Q}_{i}$ are
\[
\begin{aligned}
p_{01}&=-\dfrac{2\lambda^{2}}{35}, & p_{02}&=\lambda, & p_{03}&=-\lambda,\\
p_{12}&=\dfrac{\lambda^{3}}{5},    & p_{13}&=-\dfrac{\lambda^{3}}{7}, & p_{23}&=-\lambda^{2}. \tag{4}
\end{aligned}
\]
They satisfy $(\dagger)$ and $(\ddagger_{1})$-$(\ddagger_{3})$ by direct substitution.

\smallskip
Inside $\mathbb{P}^{5}$ the three independent linear equations cut out the projective plane  
\[
\Lambda\cong\mathbb{P}^{2}.
\]
Introduce homogeneous coordinates on $\Lambda$ by
\[
(u:v:w):=(p_{02}:p_{12}:p_{01}). \tag{5}
\]
Using $(\ddagger_{1})$-$(\ddagger_{3})$ we obtain
\[
p_{03}=-u,\qquad p_{13}=-\dfrac{5v}{7},\qquad p_{23}= \dfrac{35w}{2}. \tag{6}
\]
Substituting these expressions into $(\dagger)$ gives the quadratic
\[
\dfrac{35}{2}w^{2}-\dfrac{2}{7}uv=0, \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad 245w^{2}-4uv=0. \tag{7}
\]
Hence
\[
Z=\bigl\{(u:v:w)\in\mathbb{P}^{2}\bigm|245w^{2}-4uv=0\bigr\}. \tag{8}
\]

\textbf{Decomposition of $Z$.}  
Define
\[
C:=\bigl\{(u:v:w)\in Z\mid w\neq0\bigr\}. \tag{9}
\]
Because $w\neq0$ one can de-homogenise the quadratic equation to obtain an affine equation of a smooth conic; hence $C$ is smooth and isomorphic to $\mathbb{P}^{1}$.

The two points with $w=0$ are
\[
(1:0:0)\quad\text{and}\quad(0:1:0),
\]
corresponding (via (6)) to the Plucker points  
\[
P_{L}=(0:1:-1:0:0:0),\qquad
P_{\infty}=(0:0:0:1:-5/7:0).
\]
Thus
\[
Z=C\;\dot{\cup}\;\{P_{L}\}\;\dot{\cup}\;\{P_{\infty}\}. \tag{10}
\]
Employing $w=-2\lambda^{2}/35\neq0$ we obtain
\[
u=\lambda,\quad v=\dfrac{\lambda^{3}}{5},\quad w=-\dfrac{2\lambda^{2}}{35},
\]
which satisfies (7) and reproduces (4); consequently $C$ is exactly the
Plucker image of $\mathfrak{L}$ and no admissible line is represented by
$P_{L}$ or $P_{\infty}$.

\bigskip
\underline{\textbf{Part (iii) - The surface swept out by $\mathfrak{L}$}}

From (3) we have the relations
\[
y+z=\lambda,\qquad 5y+7z=\lambda(7-2u). \tag{11}
\]
Hence
\[
x=\dfrac{\lambda^{2}(7-2u)}{35}
     =\dfrac{(y+z)(5y+7z)}{35}, \tag{12}
\]
so every admissible line lies on
\[
S\colon x=\dfrac{(y+z)(5y+7z)}{35}. \tag{13}
\]
Conversely, let $(x,y,z)\in S$ with $(y,z)\neq(0,0)$.  Put
\[
\lambda:=y+z,\qquad u:=\dfrac{y}{\lambda}.
\]
Then $(x,y,z)$ coincides with (3).  Because $\lambda\neq0$, the point
lies on a \emph{unique} admissible line.  
Points of $L\colon x=0,\;y+z=0$ satisfy $\lambda=0$ and are not reached.  
Therefore
\[
\bigcup_{\ell\in\mathfrak{L}}\ell \;=\;S\setminus L. \tag{14}
\]

\bigskip
\underline{\textbf{Part (iv) - Hyperbolic paraboloid and the second ruling}}

Rewrite $(\star)$ as
\[
35x=(y+z)(5y+7z)=5y^{2}+12yz+7z^{2}. \tag{15}
\]
The quadratic form on the right has determinant $5\cdot7-6^{2}=-1<0$, hence $S$ is a hyperbolic paraboloid and therefore doubly ruled.

\smallskip
Fix $k\in\mathbb{R}$ and set
\[
y(t)=7t,\qquad z(t)=-5t+\dfrac{k}{7}. \tag{16}
\]
Then $5y(t)+7z(t)=k$ and $y(t)+z(t)=2t+k/7$, giving
\[
x(t)=\dfrac{(2t+k/7)k}{35}=\dfrac{2k}{35}t+\dfrac{k^{2}}{245}. \tag{17}
\]
Thus
\[
\widehat{\ell}(k)\colon
(x,y,z)=\Bigl(\dfrac{2k}{35}t+\dfrac{k^{2}}{245},\;7t,\;-5t+\dfrac{k}{7}\Bigr),
\quad t\in\mathbb{R}. \tag{18}
\]
The direction vector is $\widehat{v}=(2k/35,\,7,\,-5)$, and
$\widehat{v}\!\cdot\!(0,1,1)=2\neq0$, so no $\widehat{\ell}(k)$ is parallel to $\Pi$.  If $k\neq0$, (18) meets $\mathcal{C}_{1}$ at $t=0$ and $\mathcal{C}_{2}$ at $t=k/35$; for $k=0$ these merge to $(0,0,0)$.  Hence $\widehat{\mathfrak{L}}$ is the second ruling of $S$ and contains no admissible line.

\bigskip
\underline{\textbf{Part (v) - Differential-geometric properties}}

Put
\[
r(y,z)=\bigl(f(y,z),y,z\bigr),\qquad
f(y,z)=\dfrac{(y+z)(5y+7z)}{35}. \tag{19}
\]
\textbf{First derivatives.}  
\[
f_{y}=\dfrac{10y+12z}{35},\qquad 
f_{z}=\dfrac{12y+14z}{35}.
\]
Set $a:=f_{y}$, $b:=f_{z}$ and $W^{2}:=1+a^{2}+b^{2}$.

First fundamental form  
\[
E=1+a^{2},\qquad F=ab,\qquad G=1+b^{2}. \tag{20}
\]

\textbf{Second derivatives.}  
\[
f_{yy}=\dfrac{2}{7},\qquad f_{yz}=\dfrac{12}{35},\qquad f_{zz}=\dfrac{2}{5}. \tag{21}
\]

Second fundamental coefficients (with unit normal $N=(1,-a,-b)/W$):
\[
e=\dfrac{f_{yy}}{W},\qquad 
f=\dfrac{f_{yz}}{W},\qquad 
g=\dfrac{f_{zz}}{W}. \tag{22}
\]

\textbf{Gaussian curvature.}  
\[
eg-f^{2}
=\dfrac{f_{yy}f_{zz}-f_{yz}^{2}}{W^{2}}
=-\dfrac{4}{35^{2}}\dfrac{1}{W^{2}},\qquad
EG-F^{2}=W^{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
K=\dfrac{eg-f^{2}}{EG-F^{2}}
=-\dfrac{4}{35^{2}}\dfrac{1}{W^{4}}
=-\dfrac{4}{35^{2}}\bigl(1+\|\nabla f\|^{2}\bigr)^{-2}<0\quad\forall(y,z). \tag{23}
\]

\textbf{Non-developability.}  
For a ruled surface, developability along a ruling would force $K\equiv0$ on that ruling, impossible by (23).  Thus neither ruling is developable and $S$ is nowhere developable.

\textbf{Mean curvature.}  For a graph $(x,y,z)=(f(u,v),u,v)$
\[
H=\dfrac{(1+b^{2})f_{yy}-2ab\,f_{yz}+(1+a^{2})f_{zz}}
         {2\bigl(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\bigr)^{3/2}}. \tag{24}
\]
Substituting the derivatives gives
\[
H(y,z)=\dfrac{12+7a^{2}+5b^{2}-12ab}
              {35\bigl(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\bigr)^{3/2}}. \tag{25}
\]
Clearing the denominator $35^{2}$ one finds
\[
H=0\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
5y^{2}+12yz+7z^{2}=3675. \tag{26}
\]
Because the quadratic form has determinant $-1$, (26) describes a real hyperbola in the $(y,z)$-plane.  Its image under $r$ is the space curve
\[
\Xi=\bigl\{\bigl(f(y,z),y,z\bigr)\mid5y^{2}+12yz+7z^{2}=3675\bigr\}. \tag{27}
\]
Thus the mean curvature vanishes exactly along $\Xi$; in particular $H(0,0)=12/35>0$, so $(0,0,0)$ is not a minimal point.

\hfill$\square$

\bigskip
